experiment: re-feed vs true-KV replay as an inference verifier (head-to-head)
thesis: Replay-based verifiers (DiFR 2511.20621, TOPLOC 2501.16007, SVIP, VeriLLM) reconstruct state by re-feeding the transcript; the committed signature is the decode-time logprob. Re-feed (bulk prefill) does not reproduce decode-time, so the verifier drifts from the commitment at low-margin tokens. A thaw verifier replays from the true decode-time KV (.thawkv) and matches the commitment by construction (~0 error).

config.note: prefix caching OFF so re-feed is a clean fresh prefill (the verifier condition), never a silent reuse of live decode-time KV

drift_nats.reading: most tokens have ~zero re-feed drift; it concentrates at low-margin (decision) tokens

forgery_masking.reading: fraction of tokens where re-feed drift >= the top1-top2 gap, i.e. a single-token swap to the runner-up is smaller than the verifier's own re-feed noise and cannot be detected. true-KV verifier: 0 (drift ~ 0).

headline: At a tight verification tolerance (tau=0.01 nats), a re-feed verifier falsely rejects 6.9% of genuine tokens; a true-KV (thaw receipt) verifier rejects 0%.
At low-margin decision tokens, re-feed drift masks a single-token forgery 19.8% of the time (1 in 5) — the swap hides inside the verifier's own noise; a true-KV verifier (drift ~0) catches it.

Conclusion: replay-from-re-feed verification has both a false-reject cost on genuine output and a forgery-acceptance hole at the tokens that matter; replay-from-true-KV (thaw's .thawkv inference receipt) removes both.
scope: The true-KV arm is sound by construction (decode-time logprob = the commitment; .thawkv restores it bit-identically per thaw's separate receipts). This run measures the re-feed verifier against that ground truth on fresh generations. A head-to-head against a deployed DiFR/TOPLOC implementation, and cross-model generality, are follow-ups.
